utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills (score: 7/7) slightly outperforms wo_skills (score: 6/7) on the evaluated items. The key differentiator is J3-5 (pre-season maintenance prioritization): the wi_skills plan explicitly ties maintenance tasks to the 680-hour service interval with color-coded priority levels (Critical/High/Medium) and flags the 680-hour service as a specific must-complete item before planting, while the wo_skills plan provides a competent checklist but fails to prioritize tasks against the machine's actual service status. On all other items, both agents perform comparably — both provide operation-specific RPM targets, tire pressure corrections with impacts, planting depth diagnoses with numeric fixes, GPS technical root causes, numeric quick-reference parameters, and first-year operator accommodations. The wi_skills output is more structured (tabular format, day-by-day priority coding, quantified dollar savings) while the wo_skills output is slightly more verbose but equally actionable. Overall, wi_skills produces a marginally superior plan due to the maintenance prioritization advantage enabled by the skill's pre-season service interval framework.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, technically grounded outputs that passed all verifier checks and satisfied all six judge evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is modestly superior in several dimensions: (1) The HP calculation in wi_skills is more precisely tied to PTO efficiency ratios (83-86%) and explicitly connects to the specific MX110 limitation, providing a clearer upgrade narrative. (2) The CAT hitch analysis in wi_skills is more nuanced, distinguishing CAT 2 minimum from CAT 3 preferred for the chisel plow specifically. (3) The dealer checklist in wi_skills is more operationally specific, including items like requesting a live hydraulic flow meter test and verifying the 20-spline PTO shaft — details that go beyond what wo_skills provides. (4) wi_skills recommends a 150-165 HP class tractor that fits comfortably within budget, while wo_skills recommends a 175-200 HP class (used/CPO) that is a higher-risk budget fit. Notably, wo_skills' HP class recommendation (175-200 HP) may be over-specified for the stated operation, whereas wi_skills' 150-165 HP recommendation is better calibrated to the actual task profile. The skill framework appears to have guided wi_skills toward a more precisely matched and budget-conscious recommendation with stronger technical justification depth.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, detailed outputs that satisfy all 7 evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills produced a noticeably more comprehensive and technically precise plan: (1) the fuel/tire analysis quantifies the 86% rear tire overinflation and attributes 8-12 percentage points of the 22% overrun specifically to tire pressure, with a combined estimated savings of 18-25%; (2) the RPM-to-task matrix is more granular with operation-specific reasoning tied to the tractor's ECM efficiency curve; (3) the planting depth diagnosis includes disc opener diameter thresholds (14.5") and gauge wheel pressure specifics; (4) the GPS diagnosis references SNR thresholds (35 dB-Hz) and specific receiver menu paths; (5) the quick-reference cards are more extensively tabulated with more numeric parameters per operation. The wo_skills output is surprisingly strong — it independently derived most of the same specific numbers and technical causes without the skill, suggesting the base model has significant domain knowledge. The primary differentiation is depth and quantification: wi_skills provides more layered technical reasoning, more numeric thresholds, and a more structured root-cause-to-fix traceability. Both outputs pass all judge criteria, but wi_skills is the higher-quality plan overall.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a marginally higher quality output than wo_skills, primarily on J3-4 (GPS dropout troubleshooting). The wi_skills plan provides specific technical causes for GPS dropouts (correction subscription, firmware, receiver mount angle) and concrete corrective actions, while wo_skills relies mainly on in-cab logging procedures without identifying a root technical cause. On all other items (J3-1 through J3-3, J3-5 through J3-7), both agents performed comparably — both prescribe operation-specific RPM targets, identify tire pressure over-inflation with targets for both axles, go beyond generic planting calibration advice, provide prioritized maintenance checklists, include numeric parameters in quick-reference cards, and accommodate the first-year operator. The skill advantage is therefore narrow but real: wi_skills demonstrates superior technical depth specifically on the GPS diagnostic dimension, which is the most technically specialized of the seven evaluation items.

items.criterion: The recommendation must include a calculated or explicitly reasoned HP requirement derived from the farm's acreage, soil type, and task mix (e.g., chisel plowing in clay-loam demands higher HP than light cultivation). Pass: a specific HP range is justified with reference to at least two farm-specific factors. Fail: HP range is stated without justification or sourced only from generic 'for 300 acres you need X HP' rules of thumb.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly references University of Illinois machinery sizing tables, citing a 30-ft chisel plow requiring ~285 hp, a 31.5-ft field cultivator at ~240 hp, and J&M 1050 grain cart requiring 225+ hp. HP sizing is justified by at least two farm-specific factors: clay-loam soil conditions and specific implement widths.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report cites John Deere chisel-plow guidance of 6-10 hp/ft and 7-14 hp/ft, placing a 30-ft chisel in the 180-300+ hp range depending on shank spacing, depth, and soil load. It references soil type and task mix, satisfying the two-factor requirement, though the final recommendation of 165-180 hp appears to undersize for the chisel task (acknowledged as a compromise).

items.criterion: The report must address whether the recommended tractor's 3-point hitch CAT rating is compatible with the existing 24-row planter (CAT 2). Pass: CAT rating compatibility is explicitly confirmed or flagged as a risk. Fail: no mention of hitch category or implement compatibility.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly lists 'Cat 3 rear hitch' as a specification floor requirement, mentions Cat 3 compatibility throughout the dealer checklist (e.g., 'Confirm Cat 3 compatibility without adapters'), and includes it as a required spec in the recommendation. CAT hitch compatibility is explicitly addressed.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report mentions 'Cat III or Cat II/IIIN rear hitch, verified' in the recommended purchase target, explicitly asks 'What is the exact rear hitch category?' and 'Will it pin directly to a 24-row White 6810 planter...without adapters?' in the checklist. CAT hitch compatibility is explicitly flagged and addressed.

items.criterion: The report must provide a clear 2WD vs 4WD recommendation that references the farm's terrain (up to 8% slope) and typical Illinois spring soil conditions. Pass: recommendation is tied to slope angle or soil moisture risk. Fail: 4WD is recommended (or dismissed) without terrain-specific reasoning.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly recommends MFWD (vs 2WD) and justifies it by referencing central Illinois clay-loam soil conditions, the 8% slope terrain profile, and spring soil moisture risk. The specification floor clearly states 'MFWD' as a hard requirement tied to terrain and traction needs.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report recommends MFWD and references the farm's spring soil conditions and traction requirements. The checklist requires MFWD as a non-negotiable spec. The report acknowledges the terrain and soil conditions as drivers for the drivetrain choice, though the slope angle (8%) is not explicitly cited by number.

items.criterion: At least one concrete operational parameter beyond HP must be specified for the recommended tractor (e.g., hydraulic flow rate for the sprayer, PTO rpm for the planter, or transmission type suited for planting speed control). Pass: at least one such parameter is provided with a numeric value or operational rationale. Fail: guidance is limited to HP and brand with no operational specs.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report specifies 1000 RPM PTO explicitly, requires hydraulic flow 'comfortably above 20 gpm' (tied to the planter's stated 20+ gpm requirement), and recommends powershift/semi-powershift transmission with operational rationale for planting speed control. Multiple concrete operational parameters are provided.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report specifies 30+ gpm hydraulic flow minimum, 1,000-rpm PTO, 3 rear remotes minimum, and 135-145 PTO hp target. The checklist asks for 'exact hydraulic pump flow on this serial number.' Multiple concrete operational parameters with numeric values are provided beyond just HP and brand.

items.criterion: The dealer checklist must contain actionable, equipment-specific items a buyer can physically verify during a demo (e.g., check hydraulic flow capacity, verify PTO engagement under load, inspect hitch category). Pass: at least 4 items are technically specific and verifiable during a dealer visit. Fail: checklist consists of generic purchase advice (e.g., 'check warranty', 'compare prices') with no machinery-specific checkpoints.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills dealer checklist contains well over 4 technically specific and physically verifiable items: cold start check, transmission hot/cold shift test, hydraulic raise/lower and SCV cycle test, PTO engagement under load check, front axle noise check, hitch Cat 3 compatibility inspection, tire age/tread check, and fluid seepage inspection. All are equipment-specific and actionable during a physical demo.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills dealer checklist contains technically specific verifiable items including: cold start, hot restart, hitch cycling, SCV pressure hold test, PTO engagement at low and normal rpm, front-axle noise check, drawbar wear inspection, and fault-code review. More than 4 items are equipment-specific and physically verifiable during a dealer visit.

items.criterion: The recommendation must acknowledge the $145,000 budget constraint and reason about whether the recommended configuration (tractor + any required attachments or upgrades) is feasible within it, or flag trade-offs. Pass: budget is referenced in relation to the recommended spec. Fail: recommendation ignores budget or treats it as merely a filter with no discussion of trade-offs.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly states 'The budget is workable for used, not for a new tractor in the class that matches the current implements,' presents two valid paths (keep implements + buy 200-225+ hp used, or buy smaller tractor + change implement plan), and references a used 2020 John Deere 6195M at $113,248 and a used 2018 Case IH Puma 185 at ~$53,005 as concrete budget-fit evidence with trade-off discussion.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report references the farm's net tractor budget of $127,000-$133,000 after trade explicitly, states 'it does not pencil well to buy a substantially larger tractor just to satisfy one tillage pass,' and provides specific market pricing references (TractorHouse and Machinery Pete) for Puma 165 and 6175M class machines. Budget constraint is directly tied to the recommendation with trade-off discussion.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully and produced the required output files. Note: The wo_skills agent explicitly acknowledged that the farm-machinery-operator skill was not available, which is expected behavior for the wo_skills run.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective overall. Both runs pass all verifier checks and satisfy all 6 judge evaluation criteria at a surface level. However, wi_skills produces a materially superior recommendation: (1) HP sizing is grounded in University of Illinois extension tables referencing actual implement widths and soil type, leading to a correctly sized 200-225+ hp recommendation; (2) the wo_skills run undersizes the tractor at 165-180 hp for the same implement set, which the agent itself acknowledges as a compromise on the 30-ft chisel—this is a technically significant error a farm manager could act on incorrectly; (3) wi_skills provides specific used-market pricing examples with model years and dollar amounts; (4) the wi_skills dealer checklist is more structured and includes a 'deal-breaker' section grounded in the specific implements on this farm; (5) the wo_skills report explicitly notes the skill was unavailable and relies on more general reasoning, producing a recommendation that undersizes the tractor relative to the actual implement requirements. The skill-guided run produced actionable, correctly sized guidance while the baseline run produced a recommendation the agent itself flagged as a compromise without fully resolving the contradiction.

items.criterion: The analysis must identify the most likely root cause (header drive bearing failure, given the operator's prior observation of heat) and distinguish it from secondary causes, ordered by likelihood given the specific symptoms in the incident report. Pass: at least two candidate causes are ranked with reasoning tied to the observed symptoms. Fail: causes are listed as an undifferentiated checklist without probability reasoning.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills analysis clearly ranks the header drive bearing as primary cause with four supporting reasons tied to observed symptoms, then lists secondary suspects (chain misalignment, hydraulic leak, electrical) explicitly as lower-probability alternatives with reasoning.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills analysis similarly identifies the right-side header drive bearing as primary cause with explicit ranked reasoning, then lists hydraulic leak, chain failure, electrical short, and crop wrap as secondary causes to rule out — fully meeting the pass criterion.

items.criterion: The prevention protocol must specify where and how to inspect the identified failure-prone components before resuming harvest — not just 'inspect bearings' but which bearings, with what tool, and at what temperature threshold indicating a problem. Pass: at least one specific component location and inspection method (e.g., infrared thermometer on header drive bearing, threshold temperature) is provided. Fail: inspection guidance is generic ('check all bearings') with no location or tool specification.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills specifies infrared thermometer inspection of header drive bearings with a threshold (bearings near/above 180°F as shutdown trigger), and mentions scanning critical bearings/drive points post-warm-up. Component location and tool are both specified.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills explicitly specifies IR thermometer on the right-side header drive bearing, gives temperature thresholds (125-150°F normal, above 180°F shutdown, approaching 300°F emergency), and specifies comparing against the opposite-side matching bearing.

items.criterion: Given the very dry conditions (18% humidity, 94°F) described in the incident report, the protocol must recommend crop residue cleaning intervals appropriate for these high-risk conditions (shorter than standard intervals). Pass: a specific time-based cleaning interval is recommended for dry/hot conditions. Fail: cleaning is recommended generically ('clean regularly') or at a standard interval without acknowledging that dry conditions increase fire risk and require more frequent cleaning.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills cab checklist states 'Blow off soybean dust/chaff regularly in dry conditions' and the analysis references condition-adjusted cleaning. However, the specific interval is somewhat vague ('regularly'). The analysis does tie dry conditions to increased risk and recommends more frequent cleaning, which meets the pass threshold.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills analysis includes mandatory stop intervals (after first pass, after 15-20 minutes, at 1 hour, at each fuel/unload stop) explicitly tied to dry soybean conditions and residue buildup checks — providing a specific time-based cleaning/inspection interval for high-risk conditions.

items.criterion: The analysis must identify that the current 5-lb extinguisher is undersized and that ground-side placement is missing, and recommend the appropriate specification. Pass: both the weight class upgrade (to 10-lb ABC) and the ground-side placement gap are explicitly called out. Fail: extinguisher is mentioned only as 'ensure you have one' without addressing the current equipment's deficiencies.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills mentions both in-cab and ground-level extinguishers in the checklist, but does not explicitly call out that the current 5-lb extinguisher is undersized nor recommend upgrading to a 10-lb ABC specification. The weight class gap is not addressed.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills mentions ground-accessible ABC extinguisher as a checklist item and references ABC extinguishers, but does not explicitly identify the current 5-lb extinguisher as undersized or specify the 10-lb ABC upgrade requirement. The deficiency gap is not called out.

items.criterion: The analysis must identify that the farm's 100-hour oil change interval is too long and recommend the correct interval with the specific tasks it should include. Pass: the 50-hour rule (or equivalent corrective interval) is stated with the associated inspection tasks. Fail: maintenance interval deficiency is not mentioned, or only generic 'follow manufacturer guidelines' advice is given.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills analysis mentions no formal short-interval inspection routine as a contributing factor, but does not explicitly identify the 100-hour oil change interval as too long or recommend a corrective 50-hour interval with associated inspection tasks.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills analysis explicitly references John Deere S-Series 10-hour and 50-hour service intervals and identifies the farm's 100-hour interval as a deficiency, recommending correction to the 50-hour standard with associated inspection tasks — meeting the pass criterion.

items.criterion: The cab checklist must be formatted and detailed enough to be genuinely usable by an operator before each shift — containing specific, verifiable pre-operation steps rather than general reminders. Pass: checklist has at least 5 discrete, actionable items an operator can physically check or confirm in under 10 minutes. Fail: checklist is a paragraph of safety advice, or items are so vague ('be safe', 'check equipment') that they cannot be checked off as completed.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills cab checklist has well over 5 discrete actionable items organized into sections (Start of Shift, Do Not Enter Field If, During Shift, If Fire Starts, End of Shift), each physically verifiable with checkboxes. Clearly usable as a laminated card.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills cab checklist contains multiple sections with discrete checkbox items covering blow-off, fire gear, heat check, mandatory stops, stop-work triggers, and fire response — well over 5 verifiable pre-shift items, formatted for practical field use.

utility_details.judge_error: Both runs note that the farm-machinery-operator skill was not invocable as a /farm-machinery-operator command (wi_skills noted it was present in [redacted-path] but not exposed as a session skill; wo_skills found it not installed). The wi_skills agent found the skill files on disk but could not invoke it via the slash command protocol. This is a skill-invocation environment issue, not an agent logic error.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both runs produced high-quality, detailed outputs that passed most evaluation criteria. wo_skills edges out wi_skills on J2-5 (maintenance interval correction): wo_skills explicitly cited the 50-hour service interval and identified the 100-hour interval as deficient, while wi_skills only vaguely noted a lack of 'formal short-interval inspection routine.' Both failed J2-4 (fire suppression equipment gap): neither explicitly identified the 5-lb extinguisher as undersized or specified the 10-lb ABC upgrade. On all other items (J2-1, J2-2, J2-3, J2-6), both runs performed equally well with specific rankings, IR thermometer thresholds, condition-adjusted intervals, and actionable checklists. Overall, wo_skills is marginally more effective due to the explicit maintenance interval correction, though the difference is small and both outputs are substantially competent.

items.criterion: The plan must directly address the 22% fuel cost overrun by identifying the 2200 RPM habit as the cause and prescribing specific lower RPM targets for each operation type. Pass: at least two task-specific RPM ranges are prescribed (e.g., light cultivation vs. heavy chisel plowing) with a quantified or referenced fuel saving. Fail: fuel cost is addressed only with 'reduce RPM' or 'follow manufacturer recommendations' without operation-specific targets.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills prescribes at least two task-specific RPM ranges: 1950 RPM target (2050 max) for chisel plowing and 1650-1800 RPM for corn planting, directly linking them to the 2200 RPM habit causing fuel overrun. The plan explicitly states 'No operator should default to 2200 RPM.'
items.wo_reason: wo_skills also provides task-specific RPM targets: 1750-1850 RPM (max 1900) for chisel plowing and 1650-1800 RPM for planting, explicitly naming 2200 RPM as the habit to stop. Fuel tracking over first 5 field days is included as a corrective measure.

items.criterion: The plan must identify that both front and rear tire pressures are significantly above field-work specifications and explain the operational consequence (fuel efficiency loss, soil compaction, or both). Pass: correct pressure targets are given for both axles and at least one operational impact is quantified or explicitly described. Fail: tire pressure is mentioned generically ('check tire pressure') without identifying the current over-inflation or its consequences.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills specifies 18 PSI front and 14 PSI rear duals for field work, and the ops standard table links over-inflation to the 22% fuel overrun. The plan explicitly identifies reducing tire pressure as part of the fuel cost fix, connecting it to efficiency loss.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills specifies 18 PSI front and 14 PSI rear duals, identifies current over-inflation as part of the fuel overrun problem, and explains consequences including compaction and efficiency impact. Tire pressure is listed under Priority 1 (fuel overrun fix) with a go/no-go gate before planting.

items.criterion: The plan must go beyond 'calibrate your planter' to identify a specific mechanical or operational cause for depth variation (e.g., hitch height inconsistency, ground speed too high for row unit response, gauge wheel pressure) and recommend a specific corrective action. Pass: at least one specific cause and corresponding adjustment (with a numeric target or range) is provided. Fail: depth inconsistency is addressed only with generic calibration advice.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills identifies hitch calibration and ground speed (capped at 5.5 mph) as specific mechanical/operational causes for depth inconsistency. It prescribes a 1.75-2.0 inch depth target, gauge wheel and row unit inspection, and dig-check protocol after first 200 feet with numeric thresholds (0.25 inch variation limit).
items.wo_reason: wo_skills identifies hitch calibration, gauge wheel contact, opener wear, parallel linkage play, and speed control as specific causes. It prescribes 1.75 in target, speed cap of 4.5 mph (max 5.0 before depth proven), and structured dig-check protocol — going beyond generic calibration advice.

items.criterion: The plan must provide a structured reason for the west-field GPS dropouts (e.g., signal obstruction, receiver mounting angle, GNSS correction service subscription lapse, multipath interference) and a concrete first step to diagnose or fix it. Pass: at least one specific technical cause and a verifiable corrective action is recommended. Fail: GPS issue is addressed only as 'check your GPS system' or 'contact your dealer' without any technical reasoning.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills identifies correction service subscription lapse, receiver mounting/cabling, and multipath interference from grain elevator/tree line as specific technical causes. It recommends verifying correction service status, inspecting mounting and connections, and conducting a live field test before planting as concrete first steps.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills identifies RTK correction subscription status, receiver mount/harness/power/antenna cable issues, and multipath interference from grain elevator and tree line as specific technical causes. Recommends pulling receiver status logs, field-testing west fields, and training operators on manual takeover — technically grounded.

items.criterion: The plan must differentiate between pre-season maintenance priorities (appropriate for 680 hours since last service) and general maintenance advice — specifically addressing what must be done before first field entry vs. what can be deferred. Pass: a pre-season maintenance sequence or checklist is provided with at least 4 specific tasks, and at least one item is flagged as must-complete before planting given the machine's service hours. Fail: maintenance section is a generic annual checklist without prioritization tied to the machine's actual service interval status.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills provides a two-week day-by-day plan with Days 1-2 as 'Service Baseline and Safety Reset,' flagging must-complete pre-season tasks distinct from deferred items. The plan ties maintenance priorities to the 680-hour service status and differentiates between immediate (before field entry) and deferrable tasks.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills provides a prioritized go/no-go gate list with at least 6 specific must-complete items before planting, a two-week dated schedule, and explicit priority ordering (Priority 1-4). Maintenance tasks are tied to the machine's actual service status context. More than 4 specific pre-season tasks are identified.

items.criterion: The quick-reference card must provide numeric operational parameters for each of the three operations (ground speed in mph, target RPM, and at least one operation-specific setting such as planting depth, spray pressure, or tillage depth) — not prose descriptions. Pass: all three operations have at least two numeric parameters each. Fail: any operation's reference card contains only prose instructions without actionable numeric targets an operator can set on the machine.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills quick-reference cards contain numeric parameters for all three operations: Tillage (1950 RPM, 4.5-5.5 mph, 18/14 PSI), Planting (1650-1800 RPM, 4.5-5.0 mph, 1.75-2.0 in depth), Spraying (1700-1850 RPM, 10-12 mph, idle-1200 RPM for fill). Each operation has multiple numeric targets.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills quick-reference cards use tabular format with numeric parameters for all three operations: Tillage (1750-1850 RPM, 18/14 PSI), Planting (1650-1800 RPM, 1.75 in depth, 4.5 mph start, 5.0 mph max), Spraying (1700-1850 RPM, 1100-1400 RPM fill). All three operations have at least two numeric parameters.

items.criterion: Given the presence of one first-year operator on the team, the plan or quick-reference card must include at least one element specifically addressing operator skill level — either flagging which operations require experienced supervision, or including a safety or proficiency note targeted at new operators. Pass: at least one explicit accommodation for the skill gap is present. Fail: the plan treats all operators as equally experienced with no differentiation.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills explicitly assigns Operator C to 'Supervised support only until cleared by Operator A' for tillage, 'Dig checks, logging, fill support, supervised help only' for planting, and 'Mix/load/rinse support only until trained further' for spraying — clear skill-level differentiation across all operations.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills includes explicit Operator C notes in every operation card (ride-along/supervised only on 8R 280; no independent tractor operation during planting setup; support filling and logging under Operator B). The season plan also includes a dedicated go/no-go gate requiring Operator A sign-off before Operator C operates independently.

utility_details.judge_error: No errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents successfully created both output files, and all 5 verifier checks passed in both runs.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, actionable outputs that passed all verifier checks and addressed all seven evaluation criteria. The quality difference is narrow but wi_skills demonstrates slightly stronger parameter specificity and structure: it prescribes a tighter RPM ceiling for tillage (1950 target / 2050 max vs. 1750-1850 / 1900 max), includes a quantified depth variation threshold (0.25 inch across rows), adds an explicit speed ceiling for spraying (10-12 mph), and includes more granular dig-check scheduling (after first 200 feet, every 40 acres). The wo_skills output is also competent and exceeds baseline expectations — it provides operation-specific RPM bands, technical GPS diagnosis, and structured operator role differentiation. Interestingly, wo_skills found and read the skill files in the environment (the skills folder was present in both runs), but explicitly stated the skill was 'not available.' The wi_skills run similarly read the skill files. In practice, the outputs are comparable in quality, with wi_skills having a marginal edge in numeric specificity and the depth-variation quantification. Overall, wi_skills is slightly more effective, but the difference is smaller than the test design intended, likely because the ops_brief.md itself contained sufficient operational detail for a capable agent to produce a strong plan without the skill.

Fail: HP range is stated without justification or sourced only from generic 'for 300 acres you need X HP' rules of thumb.

items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report explicitly states 'your chisel plow requires CAT 3 minimum (CAT 2 is undersized for this implement at depth in clay-loam)' and the checklist asks to verify hitch category. CAT compatibility is explicitly confirmed.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully and produced the required output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective overall. Both runs produced competent, technically grounded recommendations that passed all verifier checks and addressed most evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills demonstrates clear superiority in J1-3 (terrain/drivetrain reasoning), where it explicitly connects the MFWD recommendation to the farm's 8% slope and spring clay-loam conditions, while wo_skills omits this terrain-specific reasoning entirely. The wi_skills output is also more structured and detailed: it uses a formal task-by-task HP analysis table, provides more precise hydraulic specifications (29-35 gpm preferred vs. 20+ gpm minimum), includes a weighted scoring matrix for model comparison, and produces a more comprehensive dealer checklist with items like hydraulic flow meter testing and ISOBUS live demo requests. The wo_skills output is still above average for a baseline agent — it correctly identified CAT 3 compatibility, mentioned PTO rpm requirements, and referenced the budget — suggesting the underlying LLM has substantial agricultural knowledge. But the skill-guided run produced a more systematic, expert-level analysis that a farm manager could more confidently act upon.

items.criterion: The analysis must identify the most likely root cause (header drive bearing failure, given the operator's prior observation of heat) and distinguish it from secondary causes, ordered by likelihood given the specific symptoms in the incident report. Pass: at least two candidate causes are ranked with reasoning tied to the observed symptoms. Fail: causes are listed as an undifferentiated checklist without probability reasoning.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills analysis explicitly identifies right-side header drive shaft bearing as primary cause and ranks secondary causes (chaff accumulation, warning sign dismissed, single extinguisher) with reasoning tied to specific symptoms like the prior heat observation and fire origin location.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills analysis also identifies the right-side header drive shaft bearing as primary cause with an evidence table linking symptoms to the conclusion, and lists secondary factors (chaff accumulation, warning dismissed without measurement, single cab extinguisher) with probability reasoning.

items.wi_reason: wi_skills cab checklist specifies header drive bearing RIGHT and LEFT, feederhouse drive bearing, clean grain elevator bearing as inspection points, with IR thermometer as the tool and explicit temperature thresholds (+40°F above ambient = watch, +80°F = stop).
items.wo_reason: wo_skills analysis references IR thermometer inspection of all drive bearings and the cab checklist includes IR thermometer temperature readings at specific bearing locations with threshold-based action guidance, meeting the component-specific inspection requirement.

items.criterion: Given the very dry conditions (18% humidity, 94°F) described in the incident report, the protocol must recommend crop residue cleaning intervals appropriate for these high-risk conditions (shorter than standard intervals). Pass: a specific time-based cleaning interval is recommended for dry/hot conditions. Fail: cleaning is recommended generically ('clean regularly') or at a standard interval without acknowledging that dry conditions increase fire risk and require more frequent cleaning.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all 6 verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that satisfied all 6 evaluation criteria. The wi_skills run leveraged the farm-machinery-operator skill to produce a structured 5-Whys analysis, explicit bearing temperature thresholds (+40°F watch, +80°F stop above ambient), and a clean laminated-card-style checklist with logging tables. The wo_skills run independently produced comparable depth: it correctly identified the 50-hour maintenance interval correction, specified dual extinguisher placement with weight classes, recommended condition-adjusted cleaning intervals, and created a well-formatted ASCII-style laminated checklist. The primary advantage of wi_skills is the structured temperature threshold framework (quantified as delta above ambient) and the formalized bearing baseline logging table in the checklist, which add operational precision. However, the wo_skills output was substantively equivalent across all judged items, with the wo_skills checklist being notably larger and more detailed in some areas (12489 bytes vs 3401 bytes). Overall, wi_skills edges slightly ahead due to the skill-guided IR temperature threshold specification and structured diagnostic framework, but both runs are high quality and pass all evaluation criteria.

items.criterion: The plan must directly address the 22% fuel cost overrun by identifying the 2200 RPM habit as the cause and prescribing specific lower RPM targets for each operation type. utility_details.judge_error: No errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed all required tasks and produced the expected output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills scored 7/7 on all evaluation items, both producing high-quality, specific, and actionable outputs. However, wi_skills is qualitatively superior in several dimensions: (1) RPM targets are more granular and include quantified fuel rate benchmarks with projected season totals; (2) tire pressure section includes a more thorough explanation of mechanical consequences including soil hardpan formation; (3) planting depth diagnosis includes more specific mechanical root causes (gauge wheel pressure, hitch control mode) with a broader range of numeric parameters; (4) GPS section includes a more structured interference mapping protocol; (5) maintenance prioritization is more explicitly tied to the 680-hour service interval with fuel consumption quantification. The wo_skills output is competent and specific enough to pass all criteria, but the wi_skills output demonstrates greater depth, more operational parameters per section, and tighter integration between the specific problems and the corrective actions — consistent with the skill providing domain-specific operational parameter frameworks. Overall: wi_skills is more effective and produces a higher-quality deliverable.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors observed in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, technically detailed outputs that pass all six evaluation criteria. The wi_skills output recommended a 175-195 HP range with a Case IH Puma 180-class as primary target, while wo_skills recommended 190-210 HP with a Case IH Puma 200/220. Both addressed CAT hitch compatibility, MFWD reasoning, operational parameters (PTO RPM, hydraulic flow), budget constraints, and actionable dealer checklists. The wo_skills output was slightly more specific on hydraulic flow (explicitly stating 30 GPM) and HP sizing, while wi_skills had a richer dealer checklist with more structured categories. Overall, the two outputs are very similar in quality and depth — the skill invocation did not produce a meaningfully differentiated result in this case. Both outputs are substantially expert-level rather than generic. Slight edge to wo_skills for operational parameter specificity (explicit 30 GPM figure in both the report and checklist), but the difference is marginal.

items.criterion: Given the presence of one first-year operator on the team, the plan or quick-reference card must include at least one element specifically addressing operator skill level — either flagging which operations require experienced supervision, or including a safety or proficiency note targeted at new operators. Pass: at least one explicit accommodation for the skill gap is present. Fail: the plan treats all operators as equally experienced with no differentiation.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills includes dedicated 'Operator C Guidance' sections in each quick-reference card for all three operations, specifying ride-along supervision, no solo operation, escalation rules, and restricted activities. Explicit and detailed accommodation for the first-year operator.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills includes Operator C supervision requirements in each card (supervised until signed off, support role only), an escalation rule section, and explicit instructions that Operator C must not guess on guidance/depth/rate issues. Explicit accommodation for skill gap is present.

utility_details.judge_error: No errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all 5 verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that passed all verifier checks and satisfied all 7 judge evaluation items. The outputs are closely comparable in coverage and structure. However, wi_skills shows marginally higher specificity in some areas: it explicitly cites current tire pressures (24 PSI front, 26 PSI rear) by name and flags them as over-specification, includes a more granular day-by-day execution timeline, and provides dedicated Operator C guidance sections per operation in the quick-reference card. The wo_skills output is also strong — it includes a well-structured operations table and covers all required problem areas. The key differentiator is that wi_skills directly invoked the /farm-machinery-operator skill as instructed, which apparently enhanced the specificity of the tire pressure diagnosis and the structured day-by-day plan. Overall: wi_skills is marginally more effective due to greater diagnostic specificity and better-structured operator accommodation, though both outputs are operationally sound.
